notes: searches: ~11, clusters: 7. Chapter follows D'Aubigne 'History of the Reformation' book 7 almost verbatim (fns 198-240 = D'Aubigne b.7, with Wylie b.6 ch.4 at 201, Martyn at 212/231/235/238, Lenfant at 226). Most attributed quotes are Luther's/Charles's words as rendered by D'Aubigne, White's cited source; verdicts credit faithful transmission plus modern corroboration of the underlying events. Control sample (needs_verification:false): GC-08-023, GC-08-032, GC-08-133 -- all confirmed correctly flagged (no smuggled checkable fact). Anachronism case: GC-08-088 'Here I stand'.

GC-08-001.rationale: [common_knowledge] Charles V, elected 1519 and the new Holy Roman Emperor, convened his first Diet at Worms in 1521; papal emissaries (esp. Aleander) pressed him to act against the Reformation. Textbook Reformation history.

GC-08-002.rationale: Frederick the Wise declined the imperial crown himself in 1519 and used his influence to elect Charles V (conditioned on repayment of a Saxon debt); Charles was thus significantly indebted to him. Frederick consistently insisted Luther receive a hearing. Claim traces also to D'Aubigne b.6 ch.11.

sources.title: Frederick III, Elector of Saxony — Wikipedia

GC-08-003.rationale: Attributed to Frederick; quote traces to White's cited source D'Aubigne (fn 198, b.6 ch.11). Consistent with Frederick's documented insistence that Luther's writings had not been refuted and deserved a hearing.

sources.title: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation, Book 7 (White's cited source; b.6-7 context)

GC-08-004.rationale: Attributed to Frederick; traces to D'Aubigne (fn 198). Consistent with Frederick's documented request for a safe-conduct and impartial hearing for Luther.

sources.title: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation, b.6-7 (White's cited source)

GC-08-005.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Diet of Worms opened in January 1521, shortly after Charles V's accession. Textbook.

GC-08-006.rationale: The 1521 Diet of Worms was the first imperial Diet at which the German princes met the newly-elected Charles V in assembly. Corroborated by standard accounts.

GC-08-007.rationale: Charles's initial approach included an invitation for Luther to come under imperial protection with promise of discussion; this preceded the wavering that followed the excommunication bull. Traces to D'Aubigne b.6-7; consistent with the documented summons/safe-conduct process.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (summons, 6 March, safe passage)

GC-08-008.rationale: Luther suffered recurrent ill health; that he was unwell when summoned is stated in D'Aubigne (fn 199, b.7 ch.1) and is plausible and uncontradicted.

GC-08-009.rationale: Well-known Luther letter to Frederick expressing resolve to come to Worms even if carried sick, and refusing flight or recantation. Quote traces to D'Aubigne (fn 199, b.7 ch.1) and reflects the documented tenor of Luther's correspondence in early 1521.

GC-08-010.rationale: Girolamo (Hieronymus) Aleander was the papal nuncio/legate at Worms who led the drive against Luther. Textbook.

GC-08-011.rationale: Aleander urgently pressed Charles V not to allow Luther a hearing. Corroborated by standard accounts of Aleander's role.

GC-08-012.rationale: The bull of excommunication Decet Romanum Pontificem was issued 3 January 1521, immediately before the Diet convened; combined with Aleander's urging it moved Charles to hesitate over Luther's appearance.

GC-08-013.rationale: Charles briefly reversed course, writing that Luther should not come unless prepared to retract; this vacillation is documented and traces to D'Aubigne b.7.

GC-08-014.rationale: Aleander's charges against Luther (sedition, blasphemy, etc.) are attributed via D'Aubigne (fn 200, b.7 ch.1) and match the substance of his surviving dispatches and address. Faithful transmission of White's source.

GC-08-015.rationale: Attributed general remark that Aleander was moved by hatred more than piety; traces to D'Aubigne (fn 200). Consistent with reported reactions to Aleander's vehemence.

GC-08-016.rationale: Aleander's aggressive stance and the accompanying national grievances made much of the Diet more sympathetic to hearing Luther; corroborated by the estates' insistence on a hearing.

GC-08-017.rationale: Under the imperial constitution enforcement of edicts required the estates' concurrence; Charles accordingly had Aleander plead before the Diet. Consistent with the constitutional practice and D'Aubigne b.7 ch.3.

GC-08-018.rationale: This is a historian's editorializing quotation; verification target is the cited secondary source. It traces to Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.6 ch.4 (fn 201). Aleander did deliver a major set-piece address for Rome before the Diet, so the quoted framing accurately reflects the cited source and the event.

sources.title: D'Aubigne/Wylie context — Diet of Worms narrative

GC-08-019.rationale: Historian's editorializing (Wylie, fn 201) praising Aleander's eloquence with a providential framing that is Wylie's own. Faithful transmission of the cited source; the underlying fact (Aleander an able orator pleading Rome's cause) is uncontested.

GC-08-020.rationale: Frederick's councilors (notably Spalatin) attended and recorded proceedings; traces to D'Aubigne b.7. Consistent with the Saxon court's documented note-taking at the Diet.

GC-08-021.rationale: Aleander's declaration that Luther's errors warranted burning 'a hundred thousand heretics' is corroborated in standard accounts of his Diet address and traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.3.

sources.title: Luther at the Diet of Worms (Aleander's speech)

GC-08-022.rationale: Aleander's disparaging characterization of Luther's followers traces to D'Aubigne (fn 202, b.7 ch.3) and matches the reported gist of his address (calling Lutherans corrupt, ignorant, degraded, etc.).

sources.title: Luther at the Diet of Worms (Aleander disparages followers)

GC-08-023.rationale: [control sample] Interpretive homiletic reflection ('these arguments are no more conclusive now than in the Reformer's day'). No checkable historical proposition; extractor's needs_verification:false flag is correct.

GC-08-024.rationale: Theological claim (the Reformation as an ongoing process to the close of history). Not empirically testable.

GC-08-025.rationale: Theological claim about progressive revelation of 'new light' on Scripture. Not empirically testable.

GC-08-026.rationale: Aleander's address impressed the Diet, and with Luther absent no defense was offered at that session. Consistent with the sequence in which Aleander spoke before Luther was summoned.

GC-08-027.rationale: [control sample] Interpretive/providential framing ('apparent victory was the signal of defeat'; Rome's decline). Rhetorical judgment, not a checkable fact; extractor's flag is correct.

GC-08-028.rationale: Duke George of Saxony, though Luther's determined enemy, rose at the Diet and denounced the abuses of Rome, calling for reform. Corroborated by modern accounts of his role.

GC-08-029.rationale: Theological framing ('the Lord moved upon' Duke George). The underlying event (George exposing papal abuses) is verified at GC-08-028/030; the divine-agency claim is not testable.

GC-08-030.rationale: Duke George's speech denouncing clerical avarice ('money, money, money') and demanding general reform traces to D'Aubigne (fn 203, b.7 ch.4) and reflects the documented grievances he raised at Worms.

sources.title: George, Duke of Saxony — grievances at Worms

GC-08-031.rationale: Duke George was Luther's most determined princely opponent, which gave his critique of Rome added weight. Corroborated.

GC-08-032.rationale: [control sample] Supernatural claim (angels of God present at the Diet). By definition not empirically testable; extractor's needs_verification:false flag is correct.

GC-08-033.rationale: Theological claim ('the voice of One greater than Luther' heard at the Diet). Not testable.

GC-08-034.rationale: The Diet's committee compiled a list of grievances (the Centum Gravamina / '101 grievances') against Rome, presented with a demand for reform. The figure of 101 is corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — grievances totaling one hundred and one

GC-08-035.rationale: Petitioners' plea urging the emperor to undertake a general reformation traces to D'Aubigne (fn 204, b.7 ch.4) and reflects the tenor of the gravamina presented at Worms.

GC-08-036.rationale: Over Aleander's objections, Charles consented to summon Luther before the Diet. Corroborated by the standard timeline (summons of 6 March 1521).

GC-08-037.rationale: The summons was accompanied by an imperial safe-conduct guaranteeing return, delivered to Wittenberg by a herald (Caspar Sturm) commissioned to escort Luther. Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (safe passage, herald)

GC-08-038.rationale: Luther's friends feared the safe-conduct would be violated (as Huss's had been) and urged him not to go; well documented and traces to D'Aubigne b.7.

sources.title: The Road to Worms (fears for the safe-conduct)

GC-08-039.rationale: Luther's defiant reply (papists seek his death; the pope is Christ's adversary) traces to D'Aubigne (fn 205, b.7 ch.6) and matches Luther's documented rhetoric of early 1521.

GC-08-040.rationale: Luther traveled with companions besides the herald (typically listed as Amsdorf, Schurff, Peter Suaven and the friar Petzensteiner); D'Aubigne (b.7 ch.7) counts three friends. Minor numbering variance among sources; substantively supported.

GC-08-041.rationale: Melanchthon's close bond with Luther and his wish to accompany (denied, as he was needed at Wittenberg) traces to D'Aubigne b.7 and is consistent with their documented relationship.

GC-08-042.rationale: Luther's parting charge to Melanchthon ('continue to teach... labor in my stead') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 206, b.7 ch.7). Faithful transmission of White's source.

GC-08-043.rationale: Crowds of students and citizens gathered at Luther's departure from Wittenberg; traces to D'Aubigne b.7 and consistent with the popular enthusiasm documented along his route.

GC-08-044.rationale: A traveling anecdote from White's cited source D'Aubigne (b.7 ch.7): a priest held up a portrait of a martyred Italian reformer (commonly identified as Savonarola) as a warning. Minor incident faithfully drawn from the source; not independently corroborated beyond it.

GC-08-045.rationale: While en route Luther learned an imperial mandate (secured through Aleander, prior to the final Edict) had ordered confiscation of his books, proclaimed by messengers. Historical; traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.7. Distinct from the later Edict of Worms.

GC-08-046.rationale: Luther's resolve to press on despite the interdict ('though interdicted in every city, I shall go on') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 207, b.7 ch.7). Faithful to source and to Luther's documented determination.

GC-08-047.rationale: Luther was received with great enthusiasm at Erfurt on his way to Worms. Corroborated.

GC-08-048.rationale: Erfurt housed the Augustinian monastery where Luther had been a friar; his visiting his old cell during the 1521 stop is consistent with his biography and traces to D'Aubigne b.7.

sources.title: The Road to Erfurt (Luther's former Augustinian house)

GC-08-049.rationale: Luther preached at the Erfurt Augustinian church on the journey (7 April 1521), the crowd overflowing. Corroborated.

sources.title: Sermon Preached at Erfurt on the Journey to Worms (John 20:19-31)

GC-08-050.rationale: The Erfurt sermon was preached on John 20:19 ('Peace be unto you'), stressing salvation by Christ's work alone. The sermon text survives and matches the theme quoted.

GC-08-051.rationale: The continuation on faith issuing in good works is part of the same Erfurt sermon; the excerpt traces to D'Aubigne (fn 208) drawing on the surviving sermon.

sources.title: Sermon Preached at Erfurt (faith and works)

GC-08-052.rationale: Along the route people warned Luther he would be burned like Huss; the Huss parallel was a real and pervasive fear at Worms. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.7.

sources.title: The Road to Worms (Huss parallel feared)

GC-08-053.rationale: Luther's reply that he would walk through fire from Worms to Wittenberg to confess Christ traces to D'Aubigne (fn 209, b.7 ch.7); faithful transmission of White's source.

GC-08-054.rationale: There was a documented attempt (via Glapion/Sickingen) to divert Luther to the Ebernburg castle before entering Worms, ostensibly for amicable settlement. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.7 and is historically grounded.

GC-08-055.rationale: The saying 'even were there as many devils in Worms as tiles on the housetops, I would enter it' is a famous and well-attested Luther utterance, recorded by contemporaries. Traces to D'Aubigne (fn 210).

sources.title: The Road to Worms (Luther's 'devils as tiles' saying)

GC-08-056.rationale: Luther's arrival at Worms (16 April 1521) drew a large crowd, reported to be as eager as for the emperor himself. Corroborated.

sources.title: Martin Luther at the Diet of Worms (crowd as eager as for Charles V)

GC-08-057.rationale: The funeral-dirge detail is a narrative flourish from White's cited source D'Aubigne (b.7 ch.7); not independently corroborated, but faithfully drawn from the source.

GC-08-058.rationale: Luther's remark 'God will be my defense' on alighting traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.7; faithful transmission of White's source. The gist matches his documented reliance on divine help at Worms.

GC-08-059.rationale: On Luther's arrival Charles convened his council to decide how to proceed; consistent with the documented deliberation over honoring the safe-conduct. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-060.rationale: The proposal to disregard Luther's safe-conduct on the Huss/Sigismund precedent was actually raised in Charles's council; the quoted bishop's argument traces to D'Aubigne (fn 211, b.7 ch.8) and reflects the documented debate over the Constance precedent.

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (Sigismund/Huss precedent debated)

GC-08-061.rationale: Charles's insistence on keeping his promised safe-conduct is well documented (see also GC-08-105, 'blush like Sigismund'); the quoted reply traces to D'Aubigne (fn 211).

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (safe-conduct honored)

GC-08-062.rationale: The council/Diet decided Luther would be heard; the hearings followed on 17-18 April. Corroborated.

GC-08-063.rationale: Luther left Wittenberg c.2 April 1521 and arrived at Worms 16 April -- about two weeks. Corroborated.

GC-08-064.rationale: Luther's remark that many reform-minded nobles 'had all been freed by my gospel' traces to Martyn, Life and Times of Luther (fn 212). Faithful transmission of the cited source.

sources.title: Martyn, Life and Times of Luther (fn 212)

GC-08-065.rationale: Opinion at Worms was divided, some crediting Luther with divine influence and others alleging demonic; consistent with the polarized reception documented. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7.

GC-08-066.rationale: The imperial marshal (Ulrich von Pappenheim) conducted Luther to the hall, reaching it only with difficulty through the crowds. Consistent with the documented crush. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-067.rationale: The old general's encouragement ('Poor monk, poor monk...') is traditionally attributed to Georg von Frundsberg and traces to D'Aubigne (fn 213, b.7 ch.8). Faithful transmission of the cited source.

GC-08-068.rationale: Luther appeared before the Diet with Charles V presiding, surrounded by the empire's leading dignitaries. Corroborated.

GC-08-069.rationale: Historian's editorializing quotation (D'Aubigne, fn 214, b.7 ch.8): Luther's mere appearance before a tribunal that received a papally-condemned man was a symbolic humiliation of Rome. The event core (pope had condemned him; the august assembly nonetheless summoned and heard him) is factual; the 'signal victory' framing is D'Aubigne's interpretation, faithfully transmitted.

GC-08-070.rationale: The whispered scriptural encouragement is a narrative detail from White's cited source D'Aubigne (b.7 ch.8); not independently corroborated, but faithfully drawn from the source.

GC-08-071.rationale: The imperial official Johann von der Ecken put the two questions to Luther -- whether the books were his and whether he would retract. Corroborated.

GC-08-072.rationale: Luther acknowledged that the books displayed were his. Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Britannica (Luther admits books are his)

GC-08-073.rationale: On 17 April Luther requested time before answering the second question; this is well documented and the quote traces to D'Aubigne (fn 216).

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Britannica (Luther requests time)

GC-08-074.rationale: Luther was granted a day and returned on 18 April for his final answer. Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Britannica (returned next day)

GC-08-075.rationale: Luther's night of intense prayer before his final appearance is documented in the tradition and traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-076.rationale: The recorded 'prayer at Worms' traces to D'Aubigne (fn 217, b.7 ch.8) and the wider tradition; its exact wording rests on that transmission rather than an autograph, but it is a long-standing recorded prayer faithfully quoted.

GC-08-077.rationale: Theological comparison of Luther's prayer to Jacob wrestling at the Jabbok ('prevailed with God'). Interpretive/devotional, not testable.

GC-08-078.rationale: Luther prepared his defense by reviewing his writings and marshalling scriptural proofs; consistent with the documented preparation. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-079.rationale: The vow with hand on the open Bible to stay faithful even unto blood traces to D'Aubigne (fn 218, b.7 ch.8); faithful transmission of White's source.

GC-08-080.rationale: Observers noted Luther's composed bearing at his second appearance (contrasted with his more hesitant first-day demeanor). Consistent with the record. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-081.rationale: Luther's opening address, apologizing for any breach of courtly usage as one raised in a convent, traces to D'Aubigne (fn 219, b.7 ch.8) and matches surviving accounts of his 18 April speech.

GC-08-082.rationale: In his 18 April speech Luther divided his works into three classes -- devotional/doctrinal, anti-papal, and polemics against individuals -- refusing a blanket retraction. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms (three classes of books)

GC-08-083.rationale: Luther conceded that in the third class (polemics against individuals) he had been more vehement than fitting. Corroborated by the surviving speech.

sources.title: Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms (admits vehemence)

GC-08-084.rationale: Luther's offer to retract any error proven from Scripture and to burn his own books traces to D'Aubigne (fn 220) and matches the well-documented content of his speech.

sources.title: Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms

GC-08-085.rationale: Luther's warning that opposing the gospel brings disaster, with Pharaoh and the Babylonian kings as examples ('God removeth mountains'), traces to D'Aubigne (fn 220, b.7 ch.8) and matches the documented speech.

sources.title: Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms

GC-08-086.rationale: Luther delivered the speech in German and, at request, repeated it in Latin. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms (German then Latin)

GC-08-087.rationale: The Diet's spokesman (von der Ecken) rebuked Luther for not answering directly and demanded a plain answer on whether he would retract. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms (demand for a plain answer)

GC-08-088.rationale: Luther's substantive final answer -- refusing to submit his faith to popes or councils that have erred, and holding his conscience captive to Scripture, closing 'God help me. Amen.' -- is well documented. The specific closing sentence 'Here I stand, I can do no other' is regarded by modern scholarship as apocryphal: it appears in the earliest printed version but not in the notaries' on-the-spot transcripts. White reproduces the full line because her cited source D'Aubigne (fn 221, b.7 ch.8) gives it. Thus the wording matches 19th-century historiography but is questioned by modern scholarship.
GC-08-088.modern_consensus: The words 'Here I stand, I can do no other' were likely added to the printed edition and are probably not part of the verbatim spoken record; the surrounding statement is authentic.
GC-08-088.period_historiography: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation b.7 ch.8, gives the full closing line 'Here I stand, I can do no other; may God help me. Amen', as did the broadly received Protestant historiography of the 19th century.

sources.title: Legends about Luther: Imperial Diet of Worms (luther.de) — 'Here I stand' a legend added later
Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (closing line apocryphal, absent from earliest records)

GC-08-089.rationale: Accounts describe the assembly momentarily stunned after Luther's answer; a narrative characterization consistent with the dramatic moment. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-090.rationale: Charles V's dismissive remark ('This monk will never make a heretic of me') is a traditionally reported reaction traced to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8; faithful transmission of White's source.

GC-08-091.rationale: The admiring remark on Luther's courage attributed to Charles traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8; faithful transmission of the cited source.

GC-08-092.rationale: The spokesman's warning that the emperor and estates would decide how to deal with 'an incorrigible heretic' traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8 and matches the documented exchange after Luther's answer.

GC-08-093.rationale: Luther's calm reiteration ('May God be my helper, for I can retract nothing') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 222, b.7 ch.8); faithful transmission.

GC-08-094.rationale: Luther was made to withdraw while the estates deliberated, then recalled; consistent with the documented procedure. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-095.rationale: Asked again to recant, Luther gave no new answer; consistent with the record. Quote traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.8.

GC-08-096.rationale: Several princes were impressed and sympathetic to Luther's stand; consistent with the divided response documented at Worms.

GC-08-097.rationale: Frederick the Wise was pleased with Luther's performance (reportedly remarking the 'Father Doctor' spoke well) and grew firmer in protecting him. Consistent with his subsequent actions (the Wartburg). Traces to D'Aubigne b.7.

sources.title: Frederick III, Elector of Saxony — Wikipedia (protection of Luther)

GC-08-098.rationale: Aleander, alarmed by the effect of Luther's speech, redoubled efforts to secure his condemnation (drafting the Edict). Consistent with his documented role. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.9.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Aleander drafts the Edict)

GC-08-099.rationale: On 19 April, the day after Luther's answer, Charles presented a written declaration to the Diet affirming his resolve to uphold the Catholic faith of his ancestors against Luther. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (Charles's declaration)

GC-08-100.rationale: Charles's declaration -- a single monk set against all Christendom, and his vow to stake kingdoms, treasures and life to end the impiety and proceed against Luther -- is a well-documented text (his handwritten profession of faith). Quote traces to D'Aubigne (fn 223).

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (Charles's 'single monk' declaration)

GC-08-101.rationale: Despite condemning Luther, Charles insisted the safe-conduct be honored and that Luther reach home safely before proceedings. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (safe-conduct honored)

GC-08-102.rationale: Papal representatives urged violating the safe-conduct, invoking Huss, whose ashes had been cast into the Rhine at Constance (c.1415, roughly a century earlier). The Constance/Rhine detail is historically accurate; quote traces to D'Aubigne (fn 224).

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (Huss/Constance precedent)

GC-08-103.rationale: German princes, though Catholic, opposed breaking the safe-conduct, citing the calamities after Huss's death (the Hussite wars). Consistent with the documented reluctance to repeat Constance. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.9.

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (princes oppose breaking safe-conduct)

GC-08-104.rationale: Charles's reply that honor and faith should find refuge in the hearts of princes traces to D'Aubigne (fn 225, b.7 ch.9); faithful transmission of the cited source and consistent with his insistence on the safe-conduct.

GC-08-105.rationale: Charles V's remark 'I should not like to blush like Sigismund' -- alluding to Sigismund's betrayal of Huss's safe-conduct at Constance -- is well attested. The scene of Huss appealing to his safe-conduct before Sigismund traces to Lenfant (fn 226).

sources.title: The Road to Worms (Charles: 'I should not like to blush like Sigismund')

GC-08-106.rationale: Charles's resolve to imitate the example of his (Catholic) ancestors is part of his 19 April declaration. Quote traces to D'Aubigne (fn 227).

sources.title: Luther and Charles V at Worms (imitating his ancestors)

GC-08-107.rationale: A large body of German knights/nobles (reportedly some hundreds, led by figures like Sickingen) pledged to protect Luther. Consistent with the documented knightly sympathy. Traces to D'Aubigne b.7.

GC-08-108.rationale: Placards for and against Luther appeared at Worms; the reported placard bearing Ecclesiastes 10:16 ('Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 230, b.7). Faithful transmission of the cited source.

GC-08-109.rationale: Frederick the Wise kept a deliberately cautious public posture toward Luther while privately protecting him -- consistent with his well-documented strategy of plausible deniability (culminating in the Wartburg).

sources.title: Frederick III, Elector of Saxony — Wikipedia (cautious protection)

GC-08-110.rationale: Spalatin's remark that Luther's small room could not hold all his visitors traces to Martyn (fn 231). Faithful transmission of the cited source; consistent with the throng of visitors documented at Worms.

sources.title: Martyn, Life and Times of Luther (fn 231)

GC-08-111.rationale: A conciliation committee (under the Archbishop of Trier) made earnest efforts to obtain a compromise from Luther after his hearing. Corroborated.

GC-08-112.rationale: Luther's refusal ('the gospel cannot be preached without offense... I would rather give up my body, blood and life') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 232, b.7 ch.10); faithful transmission.

GC-08-113.rationale: Luther's condition -- judgment only by the standard of God's word -- traces to D'Aubigne (fn 233, b.7 ch.10) and matches his sola-scriptura stance at the conciliation talks.

GC-08-114.rationale: Luther's offer to surrender his safe-conduct and his life but never the word of God traces to D'Aubigne (fn 234, b.7 ch.10); faithful transmission.

GC-08-115.rationale: Luther's claim that every Christian is as competent a judge of faith as the pope traces to Martyn (fn 235) and matches his documented views. Faithful transmission of the cited source.

sources.title: Martyn, Life and Times of Luther (fn 235)

GC-08-116.rationale: Luther was ordered to leave Worms (departing 26 April 1521), knowing condemnation would follow. Corroborated.

GC-08-117.rationale: Luther's parting saying about breaching the pope's citadel traces to D'Aubigne (fn 236, b.7 ch.11); faithful transmission of the cited source.

GC-08-118.rationale: After departing, Luther wrote to Charles to clarify that his firmness was not rebellion; documented and traces to D'Aubigne b.7 ch.11.

GC-08-119.rationale: Luther's letter professing readiness to obey the emperor in all but the word of God traces to D'Aubigne (fn 237, b.7 ch.11); faithful transmission.

GC-08-120.rationale: Luther's return journey drew honorable receptions from ecclesiastics and civil rulers despite his excommunication; consistent with the popular enthusiasm documented. Traces to Martyn (fn 231 context).

sources.title: The Road to Worms (enthusiastic reception on the road)

GC-08-121.rationale: Luther preached on the return journey despite the prohibition (e.g., at Eisenach); documented and traces to D'Aubigne/Martyn.

GC-08-122.rationale: Luther's remark that he never pledged to chain the word of God traces to Martyn (fn 238); faithful transmission of the cited source.

sources.title: Martyn, Life and Times of Luther (fn 238)

GC-08-123.rationale: Shortly after Luther left, the emperor issued the Edict of Worms against him at the urging of the papal party (Aleander drafting it). Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Edict of Worms)

GC-08-124.rationale: The Edict's extreme language denouncing Luther (as a devil in a monk's habit) traces to Martyn (fn 239) and matches the vituperative tone of the actual edict, which Aleander drafted.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Edict's hostile characterization)

GC-08-125.rationale: The Edict of Worms outlawed Luther, forbade harboring or aiding him, ordered his arrest and delivery, and mandated destruction of his writings (and penalties for his adherents). Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Britannica (provisions of the Edict)

GC-08-126.rationale: Frederick and other Luther-friendly princes had left Worms before the Edict was formally passed (25/26 May) by a reduced Diet. Corroborated.

sources.title: Diet of Worms — Wikipedia (Edict passed after many left)

GC-08-127.rationale: Frederick the Wise arranged a staged 'abduction' on Luther's homeward road, spiriting him to Wartburg Castle for protection. Corroborated.

sources.title: Luther's Time at the Wartburg (staged kidnapping by Frederick)

GC-08-128.rationale: The seizure was made deliberately secret; Frederick kept himself ignorant of Luther's exact whereabouts for a time so he could truthfully deny knowledge. Consistent with the standard account.

sources.title: Luther's Time at the Wartburg (companions and even Frederick kept in the dark)

GC-08-129.rationale: Luther remained secluded at the Wartburg from May 1521 into the following winter (until March 1522) -- through spring, summer, autumn and winter. Corroborated.

GC-08-130.rationale: Luther's despairing cry ('no one to stand like a wall... and save Israel') traces to D'Aubigne (fn 240, b.9 ch.2); faithful transmission of the cited source and consistent with his documented Wartburg distress.

GC-08-131.rationale: At the Wartburg Luther translated the New Testament into German (the 'September Testament', published 1522). Textbook.

sources.title: Luther Bible — Wikipedia (NT translated at the Wartburg)

GC-08-132.rationale: Luther stayed at the Wartburg about ten months (4 May 1521 – 3 March 1522), 'nearly a whole year', continuing to write and publish. Corroborated.

GC-08-133.rationale: [control sample] Theological claim of a divine purpose behind Luther's withdrawal to the Wartburg beyond protection/quiet. Not empirically testable; extractor's needs_verification:false flag is correct.

GC-08-134.rationale: Theological claim about Satan diverting honor from God to human agents. Not testable.
